### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Finland](/wiki/Religion_in_Finland "Religion in Finland")

Registered members of religions groups in Finland by the Statistics Finland (2024)[[1]](#cite_note-statistics-finland-population-2024-final-1)

1. Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland (62.2%)
2. Orthodox Church (1.00%)
3. Other Christian (1.00%)
4. Other religions (0.90%)
5. Unaffiliated (34.9%)

With 3.5 million members,[[267]](#cite_note-274) the [Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_of_Finland "Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland") is Finland's largest religious body; As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Finland&action=edit), 62.2% of Finns were members of the church.[[1]](#cite_note-statistics-finland-population-2024-final-1) The Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland has seen its share of the country's population declining by roughly one percent annually in recent years. The decline has been due to both church membership resignations and falling baptism rates.[[268]](#cite_note-275)[[269]](#cite_note-276) The second largest group, accounting for 26.3% of the population[[270]](#cite_note-relig_stat-277) in 2017, has no religious affiliation. A small minority belongs to the [Finnish Orthodox Church](/wiki/Finnish_Orthodox_Church "Finnish Orthodox Church") (1.1%). Other [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") denominations and the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Finland "Catholic Church in Finland") are significantly smaller, as are the [Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Finland "History of the Jews in Finland") and other non-Christian communities (totalling 1.6%). The [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") estimated the [Muslim](/wiki/Islam_in_Finland "Islam in Finland") population at 2.7% in 2016.[[271]](#cite_note-278)

Finland's state church was the Church of Sweden until 1809. As an autonomous Grand Duchy under Russia from 1809 to 1917, Finland retained the Lutheran State Church system, and the Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland was established. After Finland had gained independence in 1917, religious freedom was declared in the constitution of 1919, and a separate law on religious freedom in 1922. Through this arrangement, the Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland gained a constitutional status as a national church alongside the Finnish Orthodox Church, whose position however is not codified in the constitution. The main Lutheran and Orthodox churches have special roles such as in state ceremonies and schools.[[272]](#cite_note-279)

In 2016, 69.3% of Finnish children were [baptized](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"),[[273]](#cite_note-280) but by 2019, this figure had declined to around 62%[[274]](#cite_note-281) and 82.3% were [confirmed](/wiki/Confirmation_(Lutheran_Church) "Confirmation (Lutheran Church)") in 2012 at the age of 15,[[275]](#cite_note-282) and over 90% of the funerals are Christian. However, the majority of Lutherans attend church only for special occasions like Christmas ceremonies, weddings, and funerals. The Lutheran Church estimates that approximately 1.8% of its members attend church services weekly.[[276]](#cite_note-283) The average number of church visits per year by church members is approximately two.[[277]](#cite_note-state2004-284)

According to a 2010 [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer") poll, 33% of Finnish citizens responded that they "believe there is a God"; 42% answered that they "believe there is some sort of spirit or life force"; and 22% that they "do not believe there is any sort of spirit, God, or life force".[[278]](#cite_note-EUROBAROMETER-285) According to ISSP survey data (2008), 8% consider themselves "highly religious", and 31% "moderately religious". In the same survey, 28% reported themselves as "agnostic" and 29% as "non-religious".[[279]](#cite_note-286)

### Health

Main article: [Healthcare in Finland](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Finland "Healthcare in Finland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Verenluovuttaja.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Verenluovuttaja.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Verenluovuttaja.tiff)

A man donating blood at [Finnish Red Cross](/wiki/Finnish_Red_Cross "Finnish Red Cross") [Blood Service](/w/index.php?title=Blood_Service&action=edit&redlink=1 "Blood Service (page does not exist)") [[fi](https://fi.wikipedia.org/wiki/Veripalvelu "fi:Veripalvelu")]

[Life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") was 79 years for men and 84.2 years for women in 2023.[[280]](#cite_note-287) The under-five mortality rate was 2.3 per 1,000 live births in 2022, ranking Finland's rate among the lowest in the world.[[281]](#cite_note-288) The [fertility rate](/wiki/Fertility_rate "Fertility rate") in 2023 stood at 1.26 children born/per woman and has been below [the replacement rate](/wiki/Sub-replacement_fertility "Sub-replacement fertility") of 2.1 since 1969.[[282]](#cite_note-stat.fi-289) With a low birth rate women also become mothers at a later age, the mean age at first live birth being 30.3 in 2023.[[282]](#cite_note-stat.fi-289) A 2011 study published in *[The Lancet](/wiki/The_Lancet "The Lancet")* medical journal found that Finland had the lowest [stillbirth](/wiki/Stillbirth "Stillbirth") rate out of 193 countries.[[283]](#cite_note-290)

There has been a slight increase or no change in welfare and health inequalities between population groups in the 21st century. Lifestyle-related diseases are on the rise. More than half a million Finns suffer from [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes"), [type 1 diabetes](/wiki/Type_1_diabetes "Type 1 diabetes") being globally the most common in Finland. Many children are diagnosed with [type 2 diabetes](/wiki/Type_2_diabetes "Type 2 diabetes"). The number of [musculoskeletal diseases](/wiki/Musculoskeletal_disease "Musculoskeletal disease") and [cancers](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer") are increasing, although the cancer prognosis has improved. Allergies and dementia are also growing health problems in Finland. One of the most common reasons for work disability are due to mental disorders, in particular [depression](/wiki/Major_depressive_disorder "Major depressive disorder").[[284]](#cite_note-291) Without [age standardization](/wiki/Age_standardization "Age standardization"), the suicide rates were 13 per 100 000 in 2015, close to the North European average.[[285]](#cite_note-292) Age-standardized suicide rates are still among the highest among developed countries in the OECD.[[286]](#cite_note-293)

There are 307 residents for each doctor.[[287]](#cite_note-294) About 19% of health care is funded directly by households and 77% by taxation.

In April 2012, Finland was ranked second in Gross National Happiness in a report published by The Earth Institute.[[288]](#cite_note-295) Since 2012, Finland has every time ranked at least in the top 5 of world's happiest countries in the annual [World Happiness Report](/wiki/World_Happiness_Report "World Happiness Report") by the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"),[[289]](#cite_note-296)[[290]](#cite_note-297)[[291]](#cite_note-298) as well as ranking as the happiest country since 2018.[[292]](#cite_note-299)[[293]](#cite_note-300)

### Education and science

Main article: [Education in Finland](/wiki/Education_in_Finland "Education in Finland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Oodi_July_2019_2.jpg/250px-Oodi_July_2019_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oodi_July_2019_2.jpg)

[Helsinki Central Library Oodi](/wiki/Helsinki_Central_Library_Oodi "Helsinki Central Library Oodi") was chosen as the best new [public library](/wiki/Public_library "Public library") in the world in 2019.[[294]](#cite_note-301)

Most pre-tertiary education is arranged at the municipal level. Around 3 percent of students are enrolled in private schools (mostly specialist language and international schools).[[295]](#cite_note-302) Formal education is usually started at the age of 7. Primary school takes normally six years and lower secondary school three years.

The curriculum is set by the [Ministry of Education and Culture](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_and_Culture_(Finland) "Ministry of Education and Culture (Finland)") and the Education Board. Education is compulsory between the ages of 7 and 18. After lower secondary school, graduates may apply to trade schools or [gymnasiums](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)") (upper secondary schools). Trade schools offer a [vocational education](/wiki/Vocational_education "Vocational education"): approximately 40% of an age group choose this path after the lower secondary school.[[296]](#cite_note-303) Academically oriented gymnasiums have higher entrance requirements and specifically prepare for [Abitur](/wiki/Abitur "Abitur") and tertiary education. Graduation from either formally qualifies for tertiary education.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Lc3_2018_%28263682303%29_%28cropped%29.jpeg/250px-Lc3_2018_%28263682303%29_%28cropped%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Lc3_2018_(263682303)_(cropped).jpeg)

[Linus Torvalds](/wiki/Linus_Torvalds "Linus Torvalds"), the Finnish [software engineer](/wiki/Software_engineering "Software engineering") best known for creating the popular [open-source](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") kernel [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux").

In tertiary education, two mostly separate and non-interoperating sectors are found: the profession-oriented polytechnics and the research-oriented universities. Education is free and living expenses are to a large extent financed by the government through [student benefits](/wiki/Student_benefit "Student benefit"). There are 15 universities and 24 Universities of Applied Sciences (UAS) in the country.[[297]](#cite_note-304)[[298]](#cite_note-305) The [University of Helsinki](/wiki/University_of_Helsinki "University of Helsinki") is ranked 117th in the Top University Ranking of 2025.[[299]](#cite_note-306) [Other reputable universities](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Finland "List of universities in Finland") of Finland include [Aalto University](/wiki/Aalto_University "Aalto University") in [Espoo](/wiki/Espoo "Espoo"), both [University of Turku](/wiki/University_of_Turku "University of Turku") and [Åbo Akademi University](/wiki/%C3%85bo_Akademi_University "Åbo Akademi University") in [Turku](/wiki/Turku "Turku"), [University of Jyväskylä](/wiki/University_of_Jyv%C3%A4skyl%C3%A4 "University of Jyväskylä"), [University of Oulu](/wiki/University_of_Oulu "University of Oulu"), [LUT University](/wiki/LUT_University "LUT University") in [Lappeenranta](/wiki/Lappeenranta "Lappeenranta") and [Lahti](/wiki/Lahti "Lahti"), [University of Eastern Finland](/wiki/University_of_Eastern_Finland "University of Eastern Finland") in [Kuopio](/wiki/Kuopio "Kuopio") and [Joensuu](/wiki/Joensuu "Joensuu"), and [Tampere University](/wiki/Tampere_University "Tampere University").[[300]](#cite_note-307)

The [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum") ranks Finland's tertiary education No. 2 in the world.[[301]](#cite_note-308) Around 33% of residents have a tertiary degree, similar to Nordics and more than in most other OECD countries except Canada (44%), United States (38%) and Japan (37%).[[302]](#cite_note-309) In addition, 38% of Finland's population has a university or [college degree](/wiki/College_degree "College degree"), which is among the highest percentages in the world.[[303]](#cite_note-310)[[304]](#cite_note-311) Adult education appears in several forms, such as secondary evening schools, civic and workers' institutes, study centres, vocational course centres, and [folk high schools](/wiki/Folk_high_school "Folk high school").[[178]](#cite_note-LOC-184)

More than 30% of tertiary graduates are in science-related fields. Forest improvement, materials research, environmental sciences, neural networks, low-temperature physics, brain research, biotechnology, genetic technology, and communications showcase fields of study where Finnish researchers have had a significant impact.[[305]](#cite_note-innovation-312) Finland is highly productive in scientific research. In 2005, Finland had the fourth most scientific publications per capita of the OECD countries.[[306]](#cite_note-313) In 2007, 1,801 patents were filed in Finland.[[307]](#cite_note-314)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Finland](/wiki/Culture_of_Finland "Culture of Finland")

### Literature

Main articles: [Finnish literature](/wiki/Finnish_literature "Finnish literature") and [Finland-Swedish literature](/wiki/Finland-Swedish_literature "Finland-Swedish literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Tove_Jansson_1956.jpg/250px-Tove_Jansson_1956.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tove_Jansson_1956.jpg)

Writer and artist [Tove Jansson](/wiki/Tove_Jansson "Tove Jansson")

Written Finnish can be said to have existed since [Mikael Agricola](/wiki/Mikael_Agricola "Mikael Agricola") translated the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") into Finnish during the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") in the 16th century. However, few notable works of literature were produced until the 19th century, which marked the emergence of a Finnish national [Romantic Movement](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism"). This period prompted [Elias Lönnrot](/wiki/Elias_L%C3%B6nnrot "Elias Lönnrot") to collect Finnish and Karelian folk poetry and to arrange and publish them as the *[Kalevala](/wiki/Kalevala "Kalevala")*, the Finnish [national epic](/wiki/National_epic "National epic"). The era saw a rise of poets and novelists writing in Finnish, most notably the national writer [Aleksis Kivi](/wiki/Aleksis_Kivi "Aleksis Kivi") (*[The Seven Brothers](/wiki/The_Seven_Brothers "The Seven Brothers")*), as well as [Minna Canth](/wiki/Minna_Canth "Minna Canth"), [Eino Leino](/wiki/Eino_Leino "Eino Leino"), and [Juhani Aho](/wiki/Juhani_Aho "Juhani Aho"), who was nominated for the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") twelve times.[[308]](#cite_note-315)

Alongside Finnish-language literature, many authors of the national awakening wrote in Swedish. Prominent among them were the national poet [Johan Ludvig Runeberg](/wiki/Johan_Ludvig_Runeberg "Johan Ludvig Runeberg") (*[The Tales of Ensign Stål](/wiki/The_Tales_of_Ensign_St%C3%A5l "The Tales of Ensign Stål")*) and [Zachris Topelius](/wiki/Zachris_Topelius "Zachris Topelius"), whose works occupy a central place in Finland-Swedish literature.

Following Finland's independence, modernist tendencies gained prominence. This development was exemplified by the Finland-Swedish poet [Edith Södergran](/wiki/Edith_S%C3%B6dergran "Edith Södergran"), while Finnish-language authors increasingly explored national, historical, and social themes. Key figures of the early and mid-20th century include [Frans Eemil Sillanpää](/wiki/Frans_Eemil_Sillanp%C3%A4%C3%A4 "Frans Eemil Sillanpää"), who was awarded the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") in 1939, the historical novelist [Mika Waltari](/wiki/Mika_Waltari "Mika Waltari"), and [Väinö Linna](/wiki/V%C3%A4in%C3%B6_Linna "Väinö Linna"), whose works *[The Unknown Soldier](/wiki/The_Unknown_Soldier_(novel) "The Unknown Soldier (novel)")* and the *[Under the North Star trilogy](/wiki/Under_the_North_Star_trilogy "Under the North Star trilogy")* became central texts of post-war Finnish literature. From the 1950s onwards, Finnish poetry embraced modernism, beginning with [Paavo Haavikko](/wiki/Paavo_Haavikko "Paavo Haavikko").

In contemporary Finnish literature, a wide range of genres and styles coexist. Detective fiction and popular literature have enjoyed particular success, with authors such as [Ilkka Remes](/wiki/Ilkka_Remes "Ilkka Remes") and [Juha Vuorinen](/wiki/Juha_Vuorinen "Juha Vuorinen") reaching large audiences. At the same time, the Finland-Swedish writer and artist [Tove Jansson](/wiki/Tove_Jansson "Tove Jansson") has become one of Finland's most internationally recognised authors. Best known as the creator of *[The Moomins](/wiki/The_Moomins "The Moomins")*, she is among the most translated Finnish writers, and her work has had a lasting impact on Finnish and international popular culture, extending far beyond literature into visual media, museums, and other cultural forms.[[309]](#cite_note-316)[[310]](#cite_note-317)

### Visual arts, design, and architecture

See also: [Architecture of Finland](/wiki/Architecture_of_Finland "Architecture of Finland") and [Finnish art](/wiki/Finnish_art "Finnish art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Sammon_puolustus.jpg/250px-Sammon_puolustus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sammon_puolustus.jpg)

[Akseli Gallen-Kallela](/wiki/Akseli_Gallen-Kallela "Akseli Gallen-Kallela"), *[The Defense of the Sampo](/wiki/The_Defense_of_the_Sampo "The Defense of the Sampo")*, 1896, [Turku Art Museum](/wiki/Turku_Art_Museum "Turku Art Museum")

The visual arts in Finland started to form their characteristics in the 19th century when [Romantic nationalism](/wiki/Romantic_nationalism "Romantic nationalism") was rising in autonomous Finland. The best known Finnish painters, [Akseli Gallen-Kallela](/wiki/Akseli_Gallen-Kallela "Akseli Gallen-Kallela"), started painting in a [naturalist](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)") style but moved to national romanticism. Other notable painters of the era include [Pekka Halonen](/wiki/Pekka_Halonen "Pekka Halonen"), [Eero Järnefelt](/wiki/Eero_J%C3%A4rnefelt "Eero Järnefelt"), [Helene Schjerfbeck](/wiki/Helene_Schjerfbeck "Helene Schjerfbeck") and [Hugo Simberg](/wiki/Hugo_Simberg "Hugo Simberg"). In the late 20th century, the homoerotic art of Touko Laaksonen, pseudonym [Tom of Finland](/wiki/Tom_of_Finland "Tom of Finland"), found a worldwide audience.[[311]](#cite_note-318)[[312]](#cite_note-319)

Finland's best-known sculptor of the 20th century was [Wäinö Aaltonen](/wiki/W%C3%A4in%C3%B6_Aaltonen "Wäinö Aaltonen"), remembered for his monumental [busts](/wiki/Bust_(sculpture) "Bust (sculpture)") and sculptures. The works of [Eila Hiltunen](/wiki/Eila_Hiltunen "Eila Hiltunen") and [Laila Pullinen](/wiki/Laila_Pullinen "Laila Pullinen") exemplifies the [modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") in sculpture.

Finns have made major contributions to [handicrafts](/wiki/Handicraft "Handicraft") and [industrial design](/wiki/Industrial_design "Industrial design"): among the internationally renowned figures are [Timo Sarpaneva](/wiki/Timo_Sarpaneva "Timo Sarpaneva"), [Tapio Wirkkala](/wiki/Tapio_Wirkkala "Tapio Wirkkala") and [Ilmari Tapiovaara](/wiki/Ilmari_Tapiovaara "Ilmari Tapiovaara"). Finnish architecture is famous around the world, and has contributed significantly to several styles internationally, such as [Jugendstil](/wiki/Jugendstil "Jugendstil") (or [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau")), [Nordic Classicism](/wiki/Nordic_Classicism "Nordic Classicism") and [functionalism](/wiki/Functionalism_(architecture) "Functionalism (architecture)"). Among the top 20th-century Finnish architects to gain international recognition are [Eliel Saarinen](/wiki/Eliel_Saarinen "Eliel Saarinen") and his son [Eero Saarinen](/wiki/Eero_Saarinen "Eero Saarinen"). Architect [Alvar Aalto](/wiki/Alvar_Aalto "Alvar Aalto") is regarded as among the most important 20th-century designers in the world;[[313]](#cite_note-320) he helped bring functionalist architecture to Finland, but soon was a pioneer in its development towards an [organic](/wiki/Organic_architecture "Organic architecture") style.[[314]](#cite_note-321) Aalto is also famous for his work in furniture, lamps, textiles, and [glassware](/wiki/Glassware "Glassware"), which were usually incorporated into his buildings.

### Music

Main articles: [Music of Finland](/wiki/Music_of_Finland "Music of Finland") and [Sami music](/wiki/Sami_music "Sami music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/10stringkantele02.jpg/250px-10stringkantele02.jpg)](/wiki/File:10stringkantele02.jpg)

The [kantele](/wiki/Kantele "Kantele") is Finland's national and traditional instrument.

#### Folk

Finnish folk music can be divided into Nordic dance music and the older tradition of poem singing, poems from which the national epic, the *[Kalevala](/wiki/Kalevala "Kalevala")*, was created.
Much of Finland's classical music is influenced by traditional Finnish and Karelian melodies and lyrics, as comprised in the *Kalevala*. In the historical region of [Finnish Karelia](/wiki/Finnish_Karelia "Finnish Karelia"), as well as other parts of Eastern Finland, the old poem singing traditions were preserved better than in the western parts of the country. In the 19th century [Nordic folk dance music](/wiki/Nordic_folk_dance_music "Nordic folk dance music") largely replaced the kalevaic tradition. Finnish [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") has undergone a [roots revival](/wiki/Roots_revival "Roots revival") and has become a part of [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music"). The people of northern Finland, Sweden, and Norway, the [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_people "Sámi people"), are known primarily for highly spiritual songs called [joik](/wiki/Joik "Joik").

#### Classical

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Sibelius_with_notes.jpg/250px-Sibelius_with_notes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sibelius_with_notes.jpg)

The Finnish composer [Jean Sibelius](/wiki/Jean_Sibelius "Jean Sibelius") (1865–1957) was a significant figure in the history of [classical music](/wiki/European_classical_music "European classical music").

The first [Finnish opera](/wiki/Finnish_opera "Finnish opera") was written by the German-born composer [Fredrik Pacius](/wiki/Fredrik_Pacius "Fredrik Pacius") in 1852. Pacius also wrote the music to the poem [*Maamme/Vårt land* (Our Country)](/wiki/Maamme "Maamme"), Finland's [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem"). In the 1890s Finnish nationalism based on the *Kalevala* spread, and [Jean Sibelius](/wiki/Jean_Sibelius "Jean Sibelius") became famous for his vocal symphony *[Kullervo](/wiki/Kullervo_(Sibelius) "Kullervo (Sibelius)")*. In 1899 he composed *[Finlandia](/wiki/Finlandia "Finlandia")*, which played an important role in Finland gaining independence. He remains one of Finland's most popular national figures.

Alongside Sibelius, the distinct Finnish style of music was created by [Oskar Merikanto](/wiki/Oskar_Merikanto "Oskar Merikanto"), [Toivo Kuula](/wiki/Toivo_Kuula "Toivo Kuula"), [Erkki Melartin](/wiki/Erkki_Melartin "Erkki Melartin"), [Leevi Madetoja](/wiki/Leevi_Madetoja "Leevi Madetoja") and [Uuno Klami](/wiki/Uuno_Klami "Uuno Klami"). Important modernist composers include [Einojuhani Rautavaara](/wiki/Einojuhani_Rautavaara "Einojuhani Rautavaara"), [Aulis Sallinen](/wiki/Aulis_Sallinen "Aulis Sallinen") and [Magnus Lindberg](/wiki/Magnus_Lindberg "Magnus Lindberg"), among others. [Kaija Saariaho](/wiki/Kaija_Saariaho "Kaija Saariaho") was ranked the world's greatest living composer in a 2019 composers' poll.[[315]](#cite_note-322) Many Finnish musicians have achieved international success. Among them are the conductor [Esa-Pekka Salonen](/wiki/Esa-Pekka_Salonen "Esa-Pekka Salonen"), the opera singer [Karita Mattila](/wiki/Karita_Mattila "Karita Mattila") and the violinist [Pekka Kuusisto](/wiki/Pekka_Kuusisto "Pekka Kuusisto").

#### Popular music

*Iskelmä* (coined directly from the German word *[Schlager](/wiki/Schlager "Schlager")*, meaning "hit") is a traditional Finnish word for a light popular song. Finnish popular music also includes various kinds of [dance music](/wiki/Dance_music "Dance music"); [tango](/wiki/Tango_music "Tango music"), a style of [Argentine music](/wiki/Music_of_Argentina "Music of Argentina"), is also popular.[[316]](#cite_note-323) The light music in Swedish-speaking areas has more influences from Sweden. At least a couple of Finnish [polkas](/wiki/Polka "Polka") are known worldwide, such as *[Säkkijärven polkka](/wiki/S%C3%A4kkij%C3%A4rven_polkka "Säkkijärven polkka")*[[317]](#cite_note-324) and "*[Ievan polkka](/wiki/Ievan_polkka "Ievan polkka")*".[[318]](#cite_note-325)

During the 1970s, [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock") group [Wigwam](/wiki/Wigwam_(Finnish_band) "Wigwam (Finnish band)") and [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") group [Hurriganes](/wiki/Hurriganes "Hurriganes") gained respect abroad. The Finnish [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") scene produced some internationally acknowledged names including [Terveet Kädet](/wiki/Terveet_K%C3%A4det "Terveet Kädet") in the 1980s. [Hanoi Rocks](/wiki/Hanoi_Rocks "Hanoi Rocks") was a pioneering [glam rock](/wiki/Glam_rock "Glam rock") act.[[319]](#cite_note-326) Many Finnish [metal bands](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") have gained international recognition; Finland has been often called the "Promised Land of Heavy Metal" because there are more than 50 metal Bands for every 100,000 inhabitants – more than any other nation in the world.[[320]](#cite_note-327)[[321]](#cite_note-328) Modern Finnish popular music includes a number of prominent [rock musicians](/wiki/Rock_music_in_Finland "Rock music in Finland"), pop musicians, [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") musicians, [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") performers, and dance music acts.[[322]](#cite_note-329)[[323]](#cite_note-330)[[324]](#cite_note-331)[[325]](#cite_note-332)

Finland has won the [Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest "Eurovision Song Contest") once in 2006 when [Lordi](/wiki/Lordi "Lordi") won the contest with the song [''Hard Rock Hallelujah''](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Hallelujah "Hard Rock Hallelujah").[[326]](#cite_note-333) The Finnish pop artist [Käärijä](/wiki/K%C3%A4%C3%A4rij%C3%A4 "Käärijä") also got second place in the contest in 2023 with his worldwide hit song [''Cha Cha Cha''](/wiki/Cha_Cha_Cha_(K%C3%A4%C3%A4rij%C3%A4_song) "Cha Cha Cha (Käärijä song)").[[327]](#cite_note-334)[[328]](#cite_note-335)

### Cinema and television

Main articles: [Cinema of Finland](/wiki/Cinema_of_Finland "Cinema of Finland") and [Television in Finland](/wiki/Television_in_Finland "Television in Finland")

See also: [Lists of Finnish films](/wiki/Lists_of_Finnish_films "Lists of Finnish films")

[![Aki Kaurismäki in 2012](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Aki_Kaurism%C3%A4ki_at_Berlinale_2017.jpg/250px-Aki_Kaurism%C3%A4ki_at_Berlinale_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aki_Kaurism%C3%A4ki_at_Berlinale_2017.jpg)

Film director [Aki Kaurismäki](/wiki/Aki_Kaurism%C3%A4ki "Aki Kaurismäki")

In the film industry, notable modern directors include brothers [Mika](/wiki/Mika_Kaurism%C3%A4ki "Mika Kaurismäki") and [Aki Kaurismäki](/wiki/Aki_Kaurism%C3%A4ki "Aki Kaurismäki"), [Dome Karukoski](/wiki/Dome_Karukoski "Dome Karukoski"), [Antti Jokinen](/wiki/Antti_Jokinen "Antti Jokinen"), [Jalmari Helander](/wiki/Jalmari_Helander "Jalmari Helander"), and [Renny Harlin](/wiki/Renny_Harlin "Renny Harlin"). Some Finnish [drama series](/wiki/Drama_(film_and_television) "Drama (film and television)") are internationally known, such as *[Bordertown](/wiki/Bordertown_(Finnish_TV_series) "Bordertown (Finnish TV series)")*.[[329]](#cite_note-336)

One of the most internationally successful Finnish films are *[The White Reindeer](/wiki/The_White_Reindeer "The White Reindeer")*, directed by [Erik Blomberg](/wiki/Erik_Blomberg "Erik Blomberg") in 1952, which won the [Golden Globe Award for Best Foreign Film](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Foreign_Language_Film "Golden Globe Award for Best Foreign Language Film") in 1956;[[330]](#cite_note-337)[[331]](#cite_note-338) *[The Man Without a Past](/wiki/The_Man_Without_a_Past "The Man Without a Past")*, directed by Aki Kaurismäki in 2002, which was nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Foreign_Language_Film "Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film") in 2002 and won the [Grand Prix](/wiki/Grand_Prix_(Cannes_Film_Festival) "Grand Prix (Cannes Film Festival)") at the [2002 Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/2002_Cannes_Film_Festival "2002 Cannes Film Festival");[[332]](#cite_note-339) and *[The Fencer](/wiki/The_Fencer "The Fencer")*, directed by [Klaus Härö](/wiki/Klaus_H%C3%A4r%C3%B6 "Klaus Härö") in 2015, which was nominated for the [73rd Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/73rd_Golden_Globe_Awards#Film "73rd Golden Globe Awards") in the Best Foreign Language Film category as a Finnish/German/Estonian co-production.[[333]](#cite_note-340)

In Finland, the most significant films include *[The Unknown Soldier](/wiki/The_Unknown_Soldier_(1955_film) "The Unknown Soldier (1955 film)")*, directed by [Edvin Laine](/wiki/Edvin_Laine "Edvin Laine") in 1955.[[334]](#cite_note-341) *[Here, Beneath the North Star](/wiki/Here,_Beneath_the_North_Star "Here, Beneath the North Star")* from 1968, is also one of the most significant works in Finnish history.[[335]](#cite_note-342) A 1960 [crime comedy film](/wiki/Crime_comedy_film "Crime comedy film") *[Inspector Palmu's Mistake](/wiki/Inspector_Palmu%27s_Mistake_(film) "Inspector Palmu's Mistake (film)")*, directed by [Matti Kassila](/wiki/Matti_Kassila "Matti Kassila"), was voted in 2012 the best Finnish film of all time by Finnish film critics and journalists,[[336]](#cite_note-343) but the 1984 comedy film *[Uuno Turhapuro in the Army](/wiki/Uuno_Turhapuro_in_the_Army "Uuno Turhapuro in the Army")*, the ninth film in [*Uuno Turhapuro* film series](/wiki/Uuno_Turhapuro "Uuno Turhapuro"), remains Finland's most seen domestic film made since 1968 by Finnish audience.[[337]](#cite_note-344)

### Media and communications

Main article: [Mass media in Finland](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Finland "Mass media in Finland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Sanomatalo_-_Helsinki_-_13.9.2013.jpg/250px-Sanomatalo_-_Helsinki_-_13.9.2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sanomatalo_-_Helsinki_-_13.9.2013.jpg)

[Sanomatalo](/wiki/Sanomatalo "Sanomatalo") houses several offices of newspapers and radio stations.

Today, there are around 200 [newspapers](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_Finland "List of newspapers in Finland"), 320 popular magazines, 2,100 professional magazines, and 67 commercial radio stations. The largest newspaper is *[Helsingin Sanomat](/wiki/Helsingin_Sanomat "Helsingin Sanomat")*, its circulation being 339,437 as of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Finland&action=edit).[[338]](#cite_note-345) [Yle](/wiki/Yle "Yle"), the Finnish Broadcasting Company, operates five [television channels](/wiki/Public_broadcasting "Public broadcasting") and thirteen radio channels. Each year, around 12,000 book titles are published.[[339]](#cite_note-Media_moves-346)

Thanks to its emphasis on transparency and equal rights, Finland's press has been rated the freest in the world.[[340]](#cite_note-347) Worldwide, Finns, along with other Nordic peoples and the Japanese, spend the most time reading newspapers.[[341]](#cite_note-348) In regards to [telecommunication infrastructure](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_Finland "Telecommunications in Finland"), Finland is ranked third, below the United States and Singapore in the Portulan Institute's [Network Readiness Index](/wiki/Network_Readiness_Index "Network Readiness Index") (NRI) – an indicator for determining the development level of a country's information and communication technologies.[[342]](#cite_note-349)

### Sauna

Main article: [Finnish sauna](/wiki/Finnish_sauna "Finnish sauna")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Smoke_Sauna_%28395139052%29.jpg/250px-Smoke_Sauna_%28395139052%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Smoke_Sauna_(395139052).jpg)

A [smoke sauna](/wiki/Smoke_sauna "Smoke sauna") in [Ruka](/wiki/Ruka,_Finland "Ruka, Finland"), Kuusamo

The Finns' love for [saunas](/wiki/Finnish_sauna "Finnish sauna") is generally associated with Finnish cultural tradition.[[343]](#cite_note-350) Sauna is a type of dry steam bath practiced widely in Finland, which is especially evident in the strong tradition around [Midsummer](/wiki/Midsummer "Midsummer") and [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"). The word sauna is of Proto-Finnish origin (found in Finnic and Sámi languages) dating back 7,000 years.[[344]](#cite_note-351) Steam baths have been part of European tradition elsewhere as well, but the sauna has survived best in Finland, in addition to Sweden, Estonia, Latvia, Russia, Norway, and parts of the United States and Canada. Moreover, nearly all Finnish houses have either their own sauna or in multi-story apartment houses, a timeshare sauna. Municipal swimming halls and hotels have often their own saunas. The [Finnish sauna culture](/wiki/Finnish_sauna_culture "Finnish sauna culture") is inscribed on the [UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists](/wiki/UNESCO_Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_Lists "UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists").[[345]](#cite_note-352)[[346]](#cite_note-353)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Finnish cuisine](/wiki/Finnish_cuisine "Finnish cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Ruisleip%C3%A4-limppu_reik%C3%A4leip%C3%A4_reissumies_hapankorppu-1.JPG/250px-Ruisleip%C3%A4-limppu_reik%C3%A4leip%C3%A4_reissumies_hapankorppu-1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ruisleip%C3%A4-limppu_reik%C3%A4leip%C3%A4_reissumies_hapankorppu-1.JPG)

*[Ruisleipä](/wiki/Ruisleip%C3%A4 "Ruisleipä")*, a dark [sourdough](/wiki/Sourdough "Sourdough") [rye bread](/wiki/Rye_bread "Rye bread"), holds the status of the [national food](/wiki/National_food "National food") in Finland.[[347]](#cite_note-354)

Finnish cuisine generally combines traditional country fare and contemporary style cooking. Potato, meat and [fish](/wiki/Fish_(food) "Fish (food)") play a prominent role in traditional Finnish dishes. Finnish foods often use [wholemeal](/wiki/Wholemeal "Wholemeal") products ([rye](/wiki/Rye "Rye"), [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley"), [oats](/wiki/Oat "Oat")) and berries (such as [bilberries](/wiki/Bilberry "Bilberry"), [lingonberries](/wiki/Lingonberry "Lingonberry"), [cloudberries](/wiki/Cloudberry "Cloudberry"), and [sea buckthorn](/wiki/Sea_buckthorn "Sea buckthorn")). Milk and its derivatives like [buttermilk](/wiki/Buttermilk "Buttermilk") are commonly used as food and drink. The most popular fish food in Finland is [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon").[[348]](#cite_note-355)[[349]](#cite_note-356)

Finland has the world's second highest per capita consumption of [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee").[[350]](#cite_note-357) Milk consumption is also high, at an average of about 112 litres (25 imp gal; 30 US gal), per person, per year,[[351]](#cite_note-358) even though 17% of the Finns are [lactose intolerant](/wiki/Lactose_intolerant "Lactose intolerant").[[352]](#cite_note-359)

### Public holidays

Main articles: [Public holidays in Finland](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Finland "Public holidays in Finland") and [Flag flying days in Finland](/wiki/Flag_flying_days_in_Finland "Flag flying days in Finland")

There are several holidays in Finland, of which perhaps the most characteristic of Finnish culture include Christmas (*joulu*), Midsummer (*juhannus*), [May Day](/wiki/Vappu "Vappu") (*vappu*) and [Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Finland) "Independence Day (Finland)") (*itsenäisyyspäivä*). Of these, Christmas and Midsummer are special in Finland because the actual festivities take place on eves, such as [Christmas Eve](/wiki/Christmas_Eve "Christmas Eve")[[353]](#cite_note-360)[[354]](#cite_note-361) and [Midsummer's Eve](/wiki/Juhannus "Juhannus"),[[355]](#cite_note-362)[[356]](#cite_note-363) while Christmas Day and Midsummer's Day are more consecrated to rest. Other public holidays in Finland are [New Year's Day](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Day "New Year's Day"), [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday)#Finland "Epiphany (holiday)"), [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), [Easter Sunday](/wiki/Easter_Sunday "Easter Sunday") and [Easter Monday](/wiki/Easter_Monday "Easter Monday"), [Ascension Day](/wiki/Ascension_Day "Ascension Day"), [All Saints' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day") and [Saint Stephen's Day](/wiki/Saint_Stephen%27s_Day#Finland "Saint Stephen's Day"). All official holidays in Finland are established by Acts of Parliament.[[357]](#cite_note-364)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Finland](/wiki/Sport_in_Finland "Sport in Finland")

See also: [Finland at the Olympics](/wiki/Finland_at_the_Olympics "Finland at the Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Finlandhockeybronze2010WinterOlympics.jpg/250px-Finlandhockeybronze2010WinterOlympics.jpg)](/wiki/File:Finlandhockeybronze2010WinterOlympics.jpg)

Finland's [men's national ice hockey team](/wiki/Finland_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland men's national ice hockey team") is [ranked](/wiki/IIHF_World_Ranking "IIHF World Ranking") as one of the best in the world. The team has won four world championships (1995, 2011, 2019 and 2022) and one Olympic gold medal (2022).[[358]](#cite_note-365)[[359]](#cite_note-366)

Various sporting events are popular in Finland. [Pesäpallo](/wiki/Pes%C3%A4pallo "Pesäpallo"), the Finnish equivalent of American [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball"), is the [national sport](/wiki/National_sport "National sport") of Finland,[[360]](#cite_note-367)[[361]](#cite_note-368) although the most popular sport in terms of spectators is [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey").[[362]](#cite_note-369) Other popular sports include [athletics](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field"), [cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_(sport) "Cross-country skiing (sport)"), [ski jumping](/wiki/Ski_jumping "Ski jumping"), [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"), and [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball").[[363]](#cite_note-370) Association football is the most played team sport in terms of the number of players in the country.[[364]](#cite_note-371)[[365]](#cite_note-372) [Finland's national basketball team](/wiki/Finland_national_basketball_team "Finland national basketball team") has received widespread public attention.[[366]](#cite_note-373)

In terms of medals and gold medals won per capita, Finland is the best-performing country in Olympic history.[[367]](#cite_note-374) Finland first participated as a nation in its own right at the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") in 1908. At the [1912 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1912_Summer_Olympics "1912 Summer Olympics"), three gold medals were won by the original "[Flying Finn](/wiki/Flying_Finn "Flying Finn")" [Hannes Kolehmainen](/wiki/Hannes_Kolehmainen "Hannes Kolehmainen"). In the 1920s and '30s, Finnish long-distance runners dominated the Olympics, with [Paavo Nurmi](/wiki/Paavo_Nurmi "Paavo Nurmi") winning a total of nine Olympic gold medals and setting 22 official world records between 1921 and 1931. Nurmi is often considered the greatest Finnish sportsman and one of the greatest athletes of all time. The [1952 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1952_Summer_Olympics "1952 Summer Olympics") were held in Helsinki.

The [javelin throw](/wiki/Javelin_throw "Javelin throw") event has brought Finland nine Olympic gold medals, five world championships, five European championships, and 24 world records. Finland also has a notable history in [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating "Figure skating"). Finnish skaters have won 8 world championships and 13 junior world cups in synchronized skating.

Finnish competitors have achieved significant success in [motorsport](/wiki/Motorsport "Motorsport"). In the [World Rally Championship](/wiki/World_Rally_Championship "World Rally Championship"), Finland has produced eight [world champions](/wiki/List_of_World_Rally_Championship_Drivers%27_champions "List of World Rally Championship Drivers' champions"), more than any other country.[[368]](#cite_note-375) In [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One"), Finland has won the most [world championships](/wiki/List_of_Formula_One_World_Drivers%27_Champions "List of Formula One World Drivers' Champions") per capita, with [Keke Rosberg](/wiki/Keke_Rosberg "Keke Rosberg"), [Mika Häkkinen](/wiki/Mika_H%C3%A4kkinen "Mika Häkkinen") and [Kimi Räikkönen](/wiki/Kimi_R%C3%A4ikk%C3%B6nen "Kimi Räikkönen") all having won the title.[[369]](#cite_note-376)

Some of the most popular recreational sports and activities include [Nordic walking](/wiki/Nordic_walking "Nordic walking"), running, cycling and skiing. [Floorball](/wiki/Floorball "Floorball") is the most popular youth and workplace sport.[[370]](#cite_note-377)
